subject: [PATCH 3/4] iova: limit_pfn-aware augmentation for log(n) 32-bit alloc

2026-05-18.patch_summary: This patch improves the performance of 32-bit DMA allocation in a mixed-DMA-mask domain by introducing a new augmented field, __subtree_max_gap32, which bounds the search by 32-bit-clamped gap size. This allows for O(log n) search time instead of O(n), making it more efficient for 32-bit allocations. The patch also updates the iova_compute_max function to maintain both __subtree_max_gap and __subtree_max_gap32 fields in one pass, and modifies the pruning logic in __iova_search_free_gap to select between these two fields based on whether a 32-bit allocation is being performed.

reviews.author: Robin Murphy
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Robin Murphy noted that the new clamped_gap32 field is not being updated correctly in iova_compute_max(), which will lead to incorrect pruning decisions for 32-bit allocations.

The reviewer questioned the necessity of the anchor node and suggested removing it if the cached_node is no longer used, citing potential issues and optimization benefits.

Reviewer Robin Murphy expressed concerns that the patch still requires a special case for 32-bit allocations and suggested that the allocation algorithm should be able to handle this case directly without needing a separate max32_alloc_size bodge. They also mentioned that growing struct iova unnecessarily could lead to issues with iova_cache usage on busy systems.

Reviewer Robin Murphy noted that the patch does not handle DMA masks of size 33-56 bits, which are not uncommon, and suggested a general algorithm for handling such cases, involving navigating left and right subtrees based on max_gap values. Additionally, they questioned whether rbtree is still the best data structure choice.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Rik,

I'm certainly interested to see this series, as improving the allocator 
has been on my to-do list for a very long time now. It might take me a 
while to page all the details back in, but some higher-level things 
stand out already...

On 2026-05-18 7:05 pm, Rik van Riel wrote:

---

The main reason the anchor node exists is to ensure that cached_node can 
always be a valid place to start an allocation walk - if we're no longer 
using the latter, there's little point in keeping the former either, 
especially if it starts causing undesirable issues. It does also help as 
an optimisation to avoid a couple of special cases in the current 
algorithm, but again if we're completely changing the algorithm then 
those may well no longer apply either.

---

I would think that if we still need a special case for this then we 
don't really have the right allocation algorithm - I recall getting as 
far as concluding that it would be hard to do with the generic interval 
tree, but with the right subtree data I would think we ought to be able 
to walk directly from the root towards the highest free space under the 
given limit_pfn, for any value of limit_pfn. In fact I'd rather hope 
that the general case could get good enough to no longer need the 
max32_alloc_size bodge either (especially since pci_32bit_workaround 
will now already give up trying at all after the first failure).

Furthermore I'm also wary of growing struct iova any more than 
absolutely necessary, since people have sometimes complained about 
iova_cache usage being a large number on big, busy systems.

---

And what about all the 33 to 56-bit DMA masks? They're not all that 
uncommon and deserve some love too. FWIW I'd imagine a general algorithm 
should be something like:

- while pfn_lo > limit_pfn && left->max_gap >= size, go left
- while pfn_hi + size <= limit_pfn && right->max_gap >= size, go right
- if sufficient space to right, insert right and stop.
- if left->max_gap > size, go left, else go up and left
- repeat

(If anything that then leans towards maintaining a dummy node down at 
start_pfn, such that we could avoid a special case for allocating off 
the leftmost end of the tree...)

I guess another question to ask these days is perhaps whether rbtree is 
still even the best choice at all, or if something newer like maple tree 
might be able to be even better?

Thanks,
Robin.

2026-05-28.patch_summary: This patch improves the performance of 32-bit DMA allocation in a mixed-DMA-mask domain by introducing a new augmented field that bounds the search by 32-bit-clamped gap size. The new field, __subtree_max_gap32, is precomputed and stored on each iova whenever the gap_to_prev changes. This allows the augmented callbacks to remain pure functions of the node without needing domain context. As a result, for 32-bit allocations, the search time is reduced from O(n) to O(log n). The patch also updates the compute helper iova_compute_max() to update both __subtree_max_gap and __subtree_max_gap32 in one pass.
